message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  In our model of the COVID-19 epidemic, infected individuals can be of four types, according whether they are asymptomatic (
                  <jats:italic>A</jats:italic>
                  ) or symptomatic (
                  <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                  ), and use a contact tracing mobile phone app (
                  <jats:italic>Y</jats:italic>
                  ) or not (
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  ). We denote by
                  <jats:italic>f</jats:italic>
                  the fraction of
                  <jats:italic>A</jats:italic>
                  ’s, by
                  <jats:italic>y</jats:italic>
                  the fraction of
                  <jats:italic>Y</jats:italic>
                  ’s and by
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  the average number of secondary infections from a random infected individual.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>We investigate the effect of non-electronic interventions (voluntary isolation upon symptom onset, quarantining private contacts) and of electronic interventions (contact tracing thanks to the app), depending on the willingness to quarantine, parameterized by four cooperating probabilities.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  For a given ‘effective’
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  obtained with non-electronic interventions, we use nonnegative matrix theory and stopping line techniques to characterize mathematically the minimal fraction
                  <jats:italic>y</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  of app users needed to curb the epidemic. We show that under a wide range of scenarios, the threshold
                  <jats:italic>y</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  as a function of
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  rises steeply from 0 at
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  <jats:italic>=</jats:italic>
                  1 to prohibitively large values (of the order of 60 – 70% up) whenever
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  is above 1.3. Our results show that moderate rates of adoption of a contact tracing app can reduce
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  but are by no means sufficient to reduce it below 1 unless it is already very close to 1 thanks to non-electronic interventions.
                </jats:p>
title: A mathematically rigorous assessment of the efficiency of quarantining and contact tracing in curbing the COVID-19 epidemic

L. Ferretti , C. Wymant , M. Kendall , L. Zhao , A. Nurtay , L. Abeler-Dorner , M. Parker , D. Bonsall , and C. Fraser . Quantifying SARS-CoV-2 transmission suggests epidemic control with digital contact tracing. Science, 2020.

J. Hellewell , S. Abbott , A. Gimma , N. I. Bosse , C. I. Jarvis , T. W. Russell , J. D. Munday , A. J. Kucharski , W. J. Edmunds , F. Sun , et al. Feasibility of controlling COVID-19 outbreaks by isolation of cases and contacts. The Lancet Global Health, 2020.

K. Mizumoto , K. Kagaya , A. Zarebski , and G. Chowell . Estimating the asymptomatic proportion of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) cases on board the Diamond Princess cruise ship, Yokohama, Japan, 2020. Eurosurveillance, 25(10), 2020.
C. J. Mode . Multitype branching processes: theory and applications, volume 34. American Elsevier Pub. Co., 1971.

H. Nishiura , T. Kobayashi , T. Miyama , A. Suzuki , S. Jung , K. Hayashi , R. Kinoshita , Y. Yang , B. Yuan , A. R. Akhmetzhanov , et al. Estimation of the asymptomatic ratio of novel coronavirus infections (COVID-19). medRxiv, 2020.
H. Qian , T. Miao , L. Li , X. Zheng , D. Luo , and Y. Li . Indoor transmission of SARS- CoV-2. medRxiv, 2020.

reference.article-title: Epimap: Towards quantifying contact networks for understanding epidemiology in developing countries
